instruction: How does ownership work in Rust?
rationale: Rust's ownership system is unique: each value has exactly one owner, ownership transfers on assignment (move semantics), and values are dropped when the owner goes out of scope. This is NOT garbage collection — it's compile-time deterministic. Rc/Arc provide reference counting but are opt-in, not the default.

instruction: What are the borrowing rules in Rust?
must_not_contain: you can have multiple mutable references
rationale: Rust enforces at COMPILE TIME: either one mutable reference (&mut T) OR any number of immutable references (&T), never both simultaneously. This prevents data races and aliasing bugs. The borrow checker is a compile-time analysis, NOT a runtime check.

instruction: What are lifetimes in Rust and when do you need explicit annotations?
rationale: Lifetimes are compile-time annotations that tell the borrow checker how long references are valid. Most cases use lifetime elision (compiler infers). Explicit annotations needed when: multiple input references, returning references, struct fields holding references. Lifetimes have ZERO runtime cost.

instruction: How does error handling work in Rust with Result and Option?
rationale: Rust uses Result<T, E> for fallible operations and Option<T> for nullable values — NOT exceptions. The ? operator propagates errors. unwrap() panics on Err/None and should only be used when you can PROVE the value exists. panic! is for unrecoverable bugs, not normal error handling.

instruction: How do traits work in Rust and how do they differ from interfaces?
must_not_contain: traits are exactly like Java interfaces
rationale: Rust traits can have default method implementations, associated types, and const items — richer than Java interfaces. Rust has NO inheritance — composition via trait bounds replaces it. You can implement foreign traits for your types (orphan rule applies). impl Trait gives static dispatch, dyn Trait gives dynamic dispatch.

instruction: What are Send and Sync in Rust and why do they matter for concurrency?
must_not_contain: Send and Sync are runtime checks
rationale: Send means a type can be transferred to another thread. Sync means a type can be shared (referenced) across threads. These are marker traits checked at COMPILE TIME — Rust prevents data races before the program runs. Rc is NOT Send (use Arc instead). Raw pointers are not Send/Sync by default.

instruction: When is unsafe Rust appropriate and what are the rules?
rationale: unsafe unlocks 5 specific powers: dereference raw pointers, call unsafe functions, access mutable statics, implement unsafe traits, access union fields. It does NOT disable the borrow checker — ownership rules still apply. The programmer MUST uphold safety invariants. Unsafe is appropriate for FFI, performance-critical paths, and low-level abstractions.

instruction: How does async/await work in Rust?
must_not_contain: Rust has a built-in async runtime
rationale: Rust async functions return a Future that does NOTHING until polled. You need an external runtime (Tokio, async-std) to drive futures. .await suspends the current future and yields to the executor. Async is zero-cost but requires careful handling of lifetimes, Send bounds, and Pin for self-referential futures.
